Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a study routine. I'd maybe glance at materials when I remembered to, but most weeks I just kind of drifted through. Sometimes I'd have a burst of energy and study for a few hours, but then I'd go days without touching anything. It wasn't organized at all—more like reacting to whatever felt urgent in the moment.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place. I'd basically ignore things until a deadline was breathing down my neck, then scramble to catch up. Some weeks I'd study a decent amount, other weeks basically nothing. It really depended on what else was going on in my life and whether I could muster the energy to care.
Q3: Usually it was panic about a deadline or someone mentioning something in a group chat that made me realize I'd missed something. I rarely logged in just to browse or stay on top of things. It was almost always reactive—like, 'oh crap, there's something due' kind of moments. Sometimes I'd log in just to check if I'd missed anything major.
Q4: Not really, no. The LMS never became a habit for me. I'd go through phases where I'd check it more often, but it never stuck. I think I just don't have that kind of discipline with online systems. It always felt like an extra step rather than something natural.
Q5: The reading materials and PDFs were probably the most useful because I could download them and not have to keep logging back in. I prefer reading and writing things out myself, so anything I could print or save worked better for me. The videos were okay but I rarely watched them all the way through.
Q6: I pretty much ignored the discussion forums entirely—I never posted anything. They felt awkward and performative, like I'd be judged for saying something wrong. I also skipped a lot of the optional readings and supplementary videos because I was already behind on the required stuff.
Q7: If something was clearly going to be on an exam or directly connected to an assignment, I'd prioritize it. Activities that felt abstract or disconnected from my grade just didn't register as important. I needed to see the immediate payoff, otherwise my brain just wouldn't engage with it.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff basically didn't exist to me. Required activities at least had the threat of consequences, so I'd eventually get to them. Optional things felt like extras I couldn't afford time-wise, even though I probably had the time—I just didn't use it well. There was no urgency pushing me toward them.
Q9: Absolutely. Once I downloaded the PDFs and readings, I'd study from those offline. I'd take notes by hand in a notebook, reread printed materials, and sometimes look things up on other websites. A lot of my actual learning happened away from the LMS entirely.
Q10: There were definitely times I was working through concepts using my notes or discussing things with classmates in person or over text. None of that showed up anywhere online. I might spend hours reviewing my handwritten notes before an exam, but the LMS would just show I hadn't logged in for days.
Q11: Yeah, that was basically my standard approach. I'd download everything I could in one session, then work from those files for weeks. It meant my login frequency was super low even when I was actually studying. I found it easier to focus without having to navigate the platform constantly.
Q12: Mid-semester was rough. I was stressed about other things in life, felt overwhelmed by how much I'd already missed, and just kind of checked out mentally. The more I fell behind, the harder it was to jump back in. It became this cycle where avoiding the course felt easier than facing how far behind I was.
Q13: Definitely. When I was tired or stressed about other stuff, the LMS was the last thing I wanted to deal with. If I had work shifts or social obligations, coursework just got pushed aside. The platform felt like this reminder of all the things I wasn't doing, which made me avoid it even more.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times. I'd open a video or reading and just zone out completely. My eyes would be on the screen but my brain was somewhere else. I'd finish and realize I hadn't absorbed anything. It was like going through the motions without actually being present.
Q15: All the time. I'd know an assignment was important but convince myself I'd do it later, then later never came until it was too late. Part of it was anxiety—starting felt overwhelming. Part of it was just avoidance because I didn't feel confident about the material.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me moving. Without them, I'd drift indefinitely. When a deadline approached, I'd suddenly become productive, but it was always last-minute and stressful. The LMS would probably show spikes of activity right before due dates and nothing in between.
Q17: Not really. By the time I got feedback, I'd already mentally moved on to the next crisis. I rarely went back to look at comments on assignments. It felt like looking backward when I was already struggling to keep up with what was ahead. Maybe if feedback came faster it would've helped more.
Q18: The course structure felt pretty rigid, which didn't work great for my chaotic approach. When I fell behind, there wasn't an easy way to catch up. The weekly modules kept moving forward whether I was ready or not. I would've benefited from more flexibility, but I also know I might've just procrastinated more.
Q19: Having everything spread across different sections of the LMS made it confusing to figure out what I actually needed to do. Clearer priorities would've helped. On the positive side, being able to download materials made it easier for me to study in my own way, even if my engagement looked low online.
Q20: I think that could be someone who's anxious or confused, clicking around trying to figure things out but not actually producing work. Or maybe they're engaging with the content but struggling to translate that into completed assignments. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity.
Q21: That's kind of me, honestly. I did okay on the exam because I crammed effectively and I understand the material better than my attendance suggests. I studied offline a lot, talked to classmates, and pulled things together when it mattered. The LMS just doesn't capture that kind of last-minute competence.
Q22: Logs can show when you clicked on things and how long you supposedly spent there. But they can't tell if you were actually paying attention or just had a tab open. They miss offline studying, real understanding, conversations with peers, and all the mental effort that doesn't happen on a screen.
Q23: To me, being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when you're curious and thinking about concepts beyond just passing. I wasn't always there this semester—sometimes I was just surviving. But when I did engage, it was more about genuine interest than checking boxes.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show the CBT sessions I did or how my stress and anxiety affected my participation. It wouldn't capture the conversations with classmates that actually helped me learn, or the hours I spent with printed notes. It also wouldn't show that despite everything, I was surprised I managed to pull off a decent grade.
